items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: How does a fuel cell differ from a conventional battery?
items.correct: A fuel cell continuously consumes externally supplied fuel (e.g. hydrogen) and oxidant; a battery has a fixed amount of reactants that get used up.
items.misconception: Fuel cells and batteries are identical — both just store electrical energy.
items.random_wrong: Fuel cells burn fuel to generate heat which is then converted to electricity.
items.why: Students think 'fuel' implies combustion; they miss that fuel cells generate electricity directly from redox reactions, continuously refuelled.

items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: What does the atomic number tell you?
items.why: It's The number of protons. “The melting point” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which side of the periodic table are non-metals on?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Right. “Left” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the atomic number of carbon?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
